solution:
First Solution. Since
\[
t^{n}-1=\left(t-x_{1}\right)\left(t-x_{2}\right) \cdots\left(t-x_{n}\right)
\]
we see that
\[
x_{1} x_{2} \cdots x_{n}=(-1)^{n-1} .
\]

Differentiating (1) we have
\[
n t^{n-1}=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(t-x_{1}\right) \cdots\left(t-x_{i=1}\right)\left(t-x_{i+1}\right) \cdots\left(t-x_{n}\right) .
\]

Evaluating for \( t=x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n} \), we obtain
\[
\begin{array}{l}
n x_{1}{ }^{n-1}= \\
\left(x_{1}-x_{2}\right)\left(x_{1}-x_{3}\right) \cdots\left(x_{1}-x_{n-1}\right)\left(x_{1}-x_{n}\right) \\
n x_{2}{ }^{n-1}=\left(x_{2}-x_{1}\right) \\
\left(x_{2}-x_{3}\right) \cdots\left(x_{2}-x_{n-1}\right)\left(x_{2}-x_{n}\right) \\
n x_{3^{n-1}}=\left(x_{3}-x_{1}\right)\left(x_{3}-x_{2}\right) \\
\cdots\left(x_{3}-x_{n-1}\right)\left(x_{3}-x_{n}\right) \\
\vdots \\
\vdots \\
n x_{n}^{n-1}=\left(x_{n}-x_{1}\right)\left(x_{n}-x_{2}\right)\left(x_{n}-x_{3}\right) \cdots\left(x_{n}-x_{n-1}\right)
\end{array}
\]

Multiplying these equations, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
n^{n}\left(x_{1} x_{2} \cdots x_{n}\right)^{n-1} & =\prod_{i<j}\left[-\left(x_{i}-x_{j}\right)^{2}\right] \\
& =(-1)^{n(n-1) / 2} \prod_{i<j}\left(x_{i}-x_{j}\right)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Using (2) we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{i<j}\left(x_{i}-x_{j}\right)^{2} & =n^{n(-1)^{(n-1)^{2}}(-1)^{-n(n-1) / 2}} \\
& =(-1)^{(n-1)(n-2) / 2} n^{n} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Second Solution. We number the roots so that \( x_{m}=\exp (2 \pi i m / n) \). Then \( x_{n}=1 \) and \( x_{s} x_{t}=x_{s+t} \), where the subscripts are reckoned modulo \( n \).

The factorization
\[
t^{n}-1=(t-1)\left(t^{n-1}+t^{n-2}+\cdots+1\right)
\]
gives
\[
\prod_{k=1}^{n-1}\left(t-x_{k}\right)=t^{n-1}+t^{n-2}+\cdots+1
\]
and in particular
\[
\prod_{k=1}^{n-1}\left(1-x_{k}\right)=n .
\]

For a moment let \( i \) be a fixed index. Then \( \left|x_{i}-x_{j}\right|=\left|1-x_{j-i}\right| \), and therefore
\[
\prod_{j \neq i}\left|x_{i}-x_{j}\right|=\prod_{k=1}^{n-1}\left|1-x_{k}\right|=n .
\]

Multiplying these equations for \( i=1,2, \ldots, n \), we obtain
\[
\prod_{i=1}^{n} \prod_{j \neq i}\left|x_{i}-x_{j}\right|=n^{n} .
\]

Since each linear factor appears twice in this double product,
\[
\prod_{i<j}\left|x_{i}-x_{j}\right|^{2}=n^{n} .
\]

Therefore the required product is \( \lambda n^{n} \), where \( \lambda \) is a number of absolute value one. We must evaluate \( \lambda \).

If \( i<j \) and \( i+j \neq n \), then \( \left(x_{i}-x_{j}\right)^{2} \) and \( \left(x_{n-j}-x_{n-i}\right)^{2} \) are distinct factors in the product. Since they are conjugates of one another, their product is a positive real number. The factors of this type can thus be paired off so that each pair has a positive product. The remaining factors are \( \left(x_{i}-x_{n-i}\right)^{2} \) where \( 1<i<n-i \). Since \( x_{i} \) and \( x_{n-i} \) are conjugate, each of these factors is a negative real number.

Suppose that \( n \) is odd. Then there are negative factors for \( i=1,2, \ldots \), \( (n-1) / 2 \), so \( \lambda=(-1)^{(n-1) / 2}=(-1)^{(n-1)(n-2) / 2} \).
Suppose \( n \) is even. Then there are negative factors for \( i=1,2, \ldots \), \( (n-2) / 2 \), so \( \lambda=(-1)^{(n-2) / 2}=(-1)^{(n-1)(n-2) / 2} \).

Thus, in either case
\[
\prod_{i<j}\left(x_{i}-x_{j}\right)^{2}=(-1)^{(n-1)(n-2) / 2} n^{n}
\]

Remark. The product of the squares of the differences of the zeros of a polynomial is known as the discriminant of the polynomial.

descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. Since
\[
polyvar^{rootcount}-1=\left(polyvar-rootfirst\right)\left(polyvar-rootsecond\right) \cdots\left(polyvar-rootlast\right)
\]
we see that
\[
rootfirst\, rootsecond \cdots rootlast=(-1)^{rootcount-1} .
\]

Differentiating (1) we have
\[
rootcount\, polyvar^{rootcount-1}=\sum_{indexi=1}^{rootcount}\left(polyvar-rootfirst\right) \cdots\left(polyvar-rootvalue_{indexi=1}\right)\left(polyvar-rootvalue_{indexi+1}\right) \cdots\left(polyvar-rootlast\right) .
\]

Evaluating for $\,polyvar=rootfirst, rootsecond, \ldots, rootlast\,$, we obtain
\[
\begin{array}{l}
rootcount\, rootfirst^{rootcount-1}= \\
\left(rootfirst-rootsecond\right)\left(rootfirst-rootthird\right) \cdots\left(rootfirst-rootlastminusone\right)\left(rootfirst-rootlast\right) \\
rootcount\, rootsecond^{rootcount-1}=\left(rootsecond-rootfirst\right) \\
\left(rootsecond-rootthird\right) \cdots\left(rootsecond-rootlastminusone\right)\left(rootsecond-rootlast\right) \\
rootcount\, rootthird^{rootcount-1}=\left(rootthird-rootfirst\right)\left(rootthird-rootsecond\right) \\
\cdots\left(rootthird-rootlastminusone\right)\left(rootthird-rootlast\right) \\
\vdots \\
\vdots \\
rootcount\, rootlast^{rootcount-1}=\left(rootlast-rootfirst\right)\left(rootlast-rootsecond\right)\left(rootlast-rootthird\right) \cdots\left(rootlast-rootlastminusone\right)
\end{array}
\]

Multiplying these equations, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
rootcount^{rootcount}\left(rootfirst\, rootsecond \cdots rootlast\right)^{rootcount-1} &=\prod_{indexi<indexj}\left[-\left(rootindexi-rootindexj\right)^{2}\right] \\
&=(-1)^{rootcount(rootcount-1) / 2} \prod_{indexi<indexj}\left(rootindexi-rootindexj\right)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Using (2) we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{indexi<indexj}\left(rootindexi-rootindexj\right)^{2} &=rootcount^{rootcount(-1)^{(rootcount-1)^{2}}(-1)^{-rootcount(rootcount-1) / 2}} \\
&=(-1)^{(rootcount-1)(rootcount-2) / 2}\, rootcount^{rootcount} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Second Solution. We number the roots so that $\,rootindexm=\exp (2 \pi i\, indexm / rootcount)\,$. Then $\,rootlast=1\,$ and $\,rootindexs\, rootindext=rootvalue_{indexs+indext}$, where the subscripts are reckoned modulo $\,rootcount$.

The factorization
\[
polyvar^{rootcount}-1=(polyvar-1)\left(polyvar^{rootcount-1}+polyvar^{rootcount-2}+\cdots+1\right)
\]
gives
\[
\prod_{indexk=1}^{rootcount-1}\left(polyvar-rootvalue_{indexk}\right)=polyvar^{rootcount-1}+polyvar^{rootcount-2}+\cdots+1
\]
and in particular
\[
\prod_{indexk=1}^{rootcount-1}\left(1-rootvalue_{indexk}\right)=rootcount .
\]

For a moment let $\,indexi\,$ be a fixed index. Then $\,\left|rootindexi-rootindexj\right|=\left|1-rootvalue_{indexj-indexi}\right|\,$, and therefore
\[
\prod_{indexj \neq indexi}\left|rootindexi-rootindexj\right|=\prod_{indexk=1}^{rootcount-1}\left|1-rootvalue_{indexk}\right|=rootcount .
\]

Multiplying these equations for $\,indexi=1,2, \ldots, rootcount\,$, we obtain
\[
\prod_{indexi=1}^{rootcount} \prod_{indexj \neq indexi}\left|rootindexi-rootindexj\right|=rootcount^{rootcount} .
\]

Since each linear factor appears twice in this double product,
\[
\prod_{indexi<indexj}\left|rootindexi-rootindexj\right|^{2}=rootcount^{rootcount} .
\]

Therefore the required product is $\,signfactor\, rootcount^{rootcount}\,$, where $\,signfactor\,$ is a number of absolute value one. We must evaluate $\,signfactor$.

If $\,indexi<indexj\,$ and $\,indexi+indexj \neq rootcount\,$, then $\,(rootindexi-rootindexj)^{2}\,$ and $\,(rootlastminusindexj-rootlastminusindexi)^{2}\,$ are distinct factors in the product. Since they are conjugates of one another, their product is a positive real number. The factors of this type can thus be paired off so that each pair has a positive product. The remaining factors are $\,(rootindexi-rootlastminusindexi)^{2}\,$ where $\,1<indexi<rootcount-indexi$. Since $\,rootindexi\,$ and $\,rootlastminusindexi\,$ are conjugate, each of these factors is a negative real number.

Suppose that $\,rootcount\,$ is odd. Then there are negative factors for $\,indexi=1,2, \ldots , (rootcount-1) / 2\,$, so $\,signfactor=(-1)^{(rootcount-1) / 2}=(-1)^{(rootcount-1)(rootcount-2) / 2}$. Suppose $\,rootcount\,$ is even. Then there are negative factors for $\,indexi=1,2, \ldots , (rootcount-2) / 2\,$, so $\,signfactor=(-1)^{(rootcount-2) / 2}=(-1)^{(rootcount-1)(rootcount-2) / 2}$.

Thus, in either case
\[
\prod_{indexi<indexj}\left(rootindexi-rootindexj\right)^{2}=(-1)^{(rootcount-1)(rootcount-2) / 2}\, rootcount^{rootcount}
\]

Remark. The product of the squares of the differences of the zeros of a polynomial is known as the discriminant of the polynomial.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. Since
\[
meadowlark^{lanternfish}-1=\left(meadowlark-candlewick\right)\left(meadowlark-dragonfly\right) \cdots\left(meadowlark-silversand\right)
\]
we see that
\[
candlewick\, dragonfly \cdots silversand=(-1)^{lanternfish-1} .
\]

Differentiating (1) we have
\[
lanternfish\, meadowlark^{\,lanternfish-1}=\sum_{foxgloves=1}^{\,lanternfish}\left(meadowlark-candlewick\right) \cdots\left(meadowlark-\text{(term with } willowtree)\right)\cdots\left(meadowlark-silversand\right) .
\]

Evaluating for \( meadowlark=candlewick, dragonfly, \ldots, silversand \), we obtain
\[
\begin{array}{l}
lanternfish\, candlewick^{\,lanternfish-1}= \\
\left(candlewick-dragonfly\right)\left(candlewick-honeycomb\right) \cdots\left(candlewick-moonshadow\right)\left(candlewick-silversand\right) \\
lanternfish\, dragonfly^{\,lanternfish-1}=\left(dragonfly-candlewick\right) \\
\left(dragonfly-honeycomb\right) \cdots\left(dragonfly-moonshadow\right)\left(dragonfly-silversand\right) \\
lanternfish\, honeycomb^{\,lanternfish-1}=\left(honeycomb-candlewick\right)\left(honeycomb-dragonfly\right) \\
\cdots\left(honeycomb-moonshadow\right)\left(honeycomb-silversand\right) \\
\vdots \\
\vdots \\
lanternfish\, silversand^{\,lanternfish-1}=\left(silversand-candlewick\right)\left(silversand-dragonfly\right)\left(silversand-honeycomb\right) \cdots\left(silversand-moonshadow\right)
\end{array}
\]

Multiplying these equations, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
lanternfish^{\,lanternfish}\left(candlewick\, dragonfly \cdots silversand\right)^{\,lanternfish-1} & =\prod_{foxgloves<nightshade}\left[-\left(willowtree-stonebridge\right)^{2}\right] \\
& =(-1)^{lanternfish(\,lanternfish-1) / 2} \prod_{foxgloves<nightshade}\left(willowtree-stonebridge\right)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Using (2) we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{foxgloves<nightshade}\left(willowtree-stonebridge\right)^{2} & =lanternfish^{\,lanternfish(-1)^{(\,lanternfish-1)^{2}}(-1)^{-lanternfish(\,lanternfish-1) / 2}} \\
& =(-1)^{(\,lanternfish-1)(\,lanternfish-2) / 2}\, lanternfish^{\,lanternfish} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Second Solution. We number the roots so that \( x_{labyrinth}=\exp (2 \pi i\, labyrinth / lanternfish) \). Then silversand \(=1\) and \( thistledown\, feathercap = x_{goldcrest+t} \), where the subscripts are reckoned modulo \( lanternfish \).

The factorization
\[
meadowlark^{\,lanternfish}-1=(meadowlark-1)\left(meadowlark^{\,lanternfish-1}+meadowlark^{\,lanternfish-2}+\cdots+1\right)
\]
gives
\[
\prod_{ambergris=1}^{\,lanternfish-1}\left(meadowlark-x_{ambergris}\right)=meadowlark^{\,lanternfish-1}+meadowlark^{\,lanternfish-2}+\cdots+1
\]
and in particular
\[
\prod_{ambergris=1}^{\,lanternfish-1}\left(1-x_{ambergris}\right)=lanternfish .
\]

For a moment let \( foxgloves \) be a fixed index. Then \( \left|willowtree-stonebridge\right|=\left|1-x_{nightshade-foxgloves}\right| \), and therefore
\[
\prod_{nightshade \neq foxgloves}\left|willowtree-stonebridge\right|=\prod_{ambergris=1}^{\,lanternfish-1}\left|1-x_{ambergris}\right|=lanternfish .
\]

Multiplying these equations for \( foxgloves=1,2, \ldots, lanternfish \), we obtain
\[
\prod_{foxgloves=1}^{\,lanternfish} \prod_{nightshade \neq foxgloves}\left|willowtree-stonebridge\right|=lanternfish^{\,lanternfish} .
\]

Since each linear factor appears twice in this double product,
\[
\prod_{foxgloves<nightshade}\left|willowtree-stonebridge\right|^{2}=lanternfish^{\,lanternfish} .
\]

Therefore the required product is \( driftwood \, lanternfish^{\,lanternfish} \), where \( driftwood \) is a number of absolute value one. We must evaluate \( driftwood \).

If \( foxgloves<nightshade \) and \( foxgloves+nightshade \neq lanternfish \), then \( \left(willowtree-stonebridge\right)^{2} \) and \( \left(copperfield-briarpatch\right)^{2} \) are distinct factors in the product. Since they are conjugates of one another, their product is a positive real number. The factors of this type can thus be paired off so that each pair has a positive product. The remaining factors are \( \left(willowtree-briarpatch\right)^{2} \) where \( 1<foxgloves<lanternfish-foxgloves \). Since \( willowtree \) and \( briarpatch \) are conjugate, each of these factors is a negative real number.

Suppose that \( lanternfish \) is odd. Then there are negative factors for \( foxgloves=1,2, \ldots, (lanternfish-1) / 2 \), so \( driftwood=(-1)^{(lanternfish-1) / 2}=(-1)^{(lanternfish-1)(lanternfish-2) / 2} \).
Suppose \( lanternfish \) is even. Then there are negative factors for \( foxgloves=1,2, \ldots, (lanternfish-2) / 2 \), so \( driftwood=(-1)^{(lanternfish-2) / 2}=(-1)^{(lanternfish-1)(lanternfish-2) / 2} \).

Thus, in either case
\[
\prod_{foxgloves<nightshade}\left(willowtree-stonebridge\right)^{2}=(-1)^{(lanternfish-1)(lanternfish-2) / 2}\, lanternfish^{\,lanternfish}
\]

Remark. The product of the squares of the differences of the zeros of a polynomial is known as the discriminant of the polynomial.

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. Since
\[
fixedpoint^{fewnumber}-1=\left(fixedpoint-knownvalueone\right)\left(fixedpoint-knownvaluetwo\right) \cdots\left(fixedpoint-knownvaluemax\right)
\]
we see that
\[
knownvalueone knownvaluetwo \cdots knownvaluemax=(-1)^{fewnumber-1} .
\]

Differentiating (1) we have
\[
fewnumber fixedpoint^{fewnumber-1}=\sum_{wholeindex=1}^{fewnumber}\left(fixedpoint-knownvalueone\right) \cdots\left(fixedpoint-x_{i=1}\right)\left(fixedpoint-x_{i+1}\right) \cdots\left(fixedpoint-knownvaluemax\right) .
\]

Evaluating for \( fixedpoint=knownvalueone, knownvaluetwo, \ldots, knownvaluemax \), we obtain
\[
\begin{array}{l}
fewnumber\ knownvalueone^{fewnumber-1}= \\
\left(knownvalueone-knownvaluetwo\right)\left(knownvalueone-knownvaluethree\right) \cdots\left(knownvalueone-knownvalueprev\right)\left(knownvalueone-knownvaluemax\right) \\
fewnumber\ knownvaluetwo^{fewnumber-1}=\left(knownvaluetwo-knownvalueone\right) \\
\left(knownvaluetwo-knownvaluethree\right) \cdots\left(knownvaluetwo-knownvalueprev\right)\left(knownvaluetwo-knownvaluemax\right) \\
fewnumber\ knownvaluethree^{fewnumber-1}=\left(knownvaluethree-knownvalueone\right)\left(knownvaluethree-knownvaluetwo\right) \\
\cdots\left(knownvaluethree-knownvalueprev\right)\left(knownvaluethree-knownvaluemax\right) \\
\vdots \\
\vdots \\
fewnumber\ knownvaluemax^{fewnumber-1}=\left(knownvaluemax-knownvalueone\right)\left(knownvaluemax-knownvaluetwo\right)\left(knownvaluemax-knownvaluethree\right) \cdots\left(knownvaluemax-knownvalueprev\right)
\end{array}
\]

Multiplying these equations, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
fewnumber^{fewnumber}\left(knownvalueone\ knownvaluetwo\ \cdots\ knownvaluemax\right)^{fewnumber-1} & =\prod_{wholeindex<wholecounter}\left[-\left(knownvalueindexed-knownvaluerival\right)^{2}\right] \\
& =(-1)^{fewnumber(fewnumber-1) / 2} \prod_{wholeindex<wholecounter}\left(knownvalueindexed-knownvaluerival\right)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Using (2) we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{wholeindex<wholecounter}\left(knownvalueindexed-knownvaluerival\right)^{2} & =fewnumber^{fewnumber(-1)^{(fewnumber-1)^{2}}(-1)^{-fewnumber(fewnumber-1) / 2}} \\
& =(-1)^{(fewnumber-1)(fewnumber-2) / 2} fewnumber^{fewnumber} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Second Solution. We number the roots so that \( knownvaluemark=\exp (2 \pi i wholemark / fewnumber) \). Then \( knownvaluemax=1 \) and \( knownvalues\ knownvaluet=x_{s+t} \), where the subscripts are reckoned modulo \( fewnumber \).

The factorization
\[
fixedpoint^{fewnumber}-1=(fixedpoint-1)\left(fixedpoint^{fewnumber-1}+fixedpoint^{fewnumber-2}+\cdots+1\right)
\]
gives
\[
\prod_{wholestep=1}^{fewnumber-1}\left(fixedpoint-x_{wholestep}\right)=fixedpoint^{fewnumber-1}+fixedpoint^{fewnumber-2}+\cdots+1
\]
and in particular
\[
\prod_{wholestep=1}^{fewnumber-1}\left(1-x_{wholestep}\right)=fewnumber .
\]

For a moment let \( wholeindex \) be a fixed index. Then \( \left|knownvalueindexed-knownvaluerival\right|=\left|1-x_{wholecounter-wholeindex}\right| \), and therefore
\[
\prod_{wholecounter \neq wholeindex}\left|knownvalueindexed-knownvaluerival\right|=\prod_{wholestep=1}^{fewnumber-1}\left|1-x_{wholestep}\right|=fewnumber .
\]

Multiplying these equations for \( wholeindex=1,2, \ldots, fewnumber \), we obtain
\[
\prod_{wholeindex=1}^{fewnumber} \prod_{wholecounter \neq wholeindex}\left|knownvalueindexed-knownvaluerival\right|=fewnumber^{fewnumber} .
\]

Since each linear factor appears twice in this double product,
\[
\prod_{wholeindex<wholecounter}\left|knownvalueindexed-knownvaluerival\right|^{2}=fewnumber^{fewnumber} .
\]

Therefore the required product is \( fixedscale\ fewnumber^{fewnumber} \), where \( fixedscale \) is a number of absolute value one. We must evaluate \( fixedscale \).

If \( wholeindex<wholecounter \) and \( wholeindex+wholecounter \neq fewnumber \), then \( \left(knownvalueindexed-knownvaluerival\right)^{2} \) and \( \left(knownvaluediffj-knownvaluediffi\right)^{2} \) are distinct factors in the product. Since they are conjugates of one another, their product is a positive real number. The factors of this type can thus be paired off so that each pair has a positive product. The remaining factors are \( \left(knownvalueindexed-knownvaluediffi\right)^{2} \) where \( 1<wholeindex<fewnumber-wholeindex \). Since \( knownvalueindexed \) and \( knownvaluediffi \) are conjugate, each of these factors is a negative real number.

Suppose that \( fewnumber \) is odd. Then there are negative factors for \( wholeindex=1,2, \ldots \), \( (fewnumber-1) / 2 \), so \( fixedscale=(-1)^{(fewnumber-1) / 2}=(-1)^{(fewnumber-1)(fewnumber-2) / 2} \).
Suppose \( fewnumber \) is even. Then there are negative factors for \( wholeindex=1,2, \ldots \), \( (fewnumber-2) / 2 \), so \( fixedscale=(-1)^{(fewnumber-2) / 2}=(-1)^{(fewnumber-1)(fewnumber-2) / 2} \).

Thus, in either case
\[
\prod_{wholeindex<wholecounter}\left(knownvalueindexed-knownvaluerival\right)^{2}=(-1)^{(fewnumber-1)(fewnumber-2) / 2} fewnumber^{fewnumber}
\]

Remark. The product of the squares of the differences of the zeros of a polynomial is known as the discriminant of the polynomial.

garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. Since
\[
vebnlpsy^{fsmtiqro}-1=\left(vebnlpsy-rdelmpqo\right)\left(vebnlpsy-suvkherd\right) \cdots\left(vebnlpsy-ogwxyhqe\right)
\]
we see that
\[
rdelmpqo\, suvkherd \cdots ogwxyhqe=(-1)^{fsmtiqro-1} .
\]

Differentiating (1) we have
\[
fsmtiqro\, vebnlpsy^{fsmtiqro-1}=\sum_{gahovrte=1}^{fsmtiqro}\left(vebnlpsy-rdelmpqo\right) \cdots\left(vebnlpsy-qzxwvtnp_{gahovrte=1}\right)\left(vebnlpsy-qzxwvtnp_{gahovrte+1}\right) \cdots\left(vebnlpsy-ogwxyhqe\right) .
\]

Evaluating for \( vebnlpsy=rdelmpqo, suvkherd, \ldots, ogwxyhqe \), we obtain
\[
\begin{array}{l}
fsmtiqro\, rdelmpqo^{fsmtiqro-1}= \\
\left(rdelmpqo-suvkherd\right)\left(rdelmpqo-mbtznayc\right) \cdots\left(rdelmpqo-ktjrsplu\right)\left(rdelmpqo-ogwxyhqe\right) \\
fsmtiqro\, suvkherd^{fsmtiqro-1}=\left(suvkherd-rdelmpqo\right) \\
\left(suvkherd-mbtznayc\right) \cdots\left(suvkherd-ktjrsplu\right)\left(suvkherd-ogwxyhqe\right) \\
fsmtiqro\, mbtznayc^{fsmtiqro-1}=\left(mbtznayc-rdelmpqo\right)\left(mbtznayc-suvkherd\right) \\
\cdots\left(mbtznayc-ktjrsplu\right)\left(mbtznayc-ogwxyhqe\right) \\
\vdots \\
\vdots \\
fsmtiqro\, ogwxyhqe^{fsmtiqro-1}=\left(ogwxyhqe-rdelmpqo\right)\left(ogwxyhqe-suvkherd\right)\left(ogwxyhqe-mbtznayc\right) \cdots\left(ogwxyhqe-ktjrsplu\right)
\end{array}
\]

Multiplying these equations, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
fsmtiqro^{fsmtiqro}\left(rdelmpqo\, suvkherd \cdots ogwxyhqe\right)^{fsmtiqro-1} & =\prod_{gahovrte<wbzepkqd}\left[-\left(hjgrksla-pfqnbxci\right)^{2}\right] \\
& =(-1)^{fsmtiqro(fsmtiqro-1) / 2} \prod_{gahovrte<wbzepkqd}\left(hjgrksla-pfqnbxci\right)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Using (2) we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{gahovrte<wbzepkqd}\left(hjgrksla-pfqnbxci\right)^{2} & = fsmtiqro^{fsmtiqro(-1)^{(fsmtiqro-1)^{2}}(-1)^{-fsmtiqro(fsmtiqro-1) / 2}} \\
& =(-1)^{(fsmtiqro-1)(fsmtiqro-2) / 2}\, fsmtiqro^{fsmtiqro} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Second Solution. We number the roots so that \( qzxwvtnp_{rixsujab}=\exp (2 \pi i rixsujab / fsmtiqro) \). Then \( ogwxyhqe=1 \) and \( zdqumhto\, wvrcluop=qzxwvtnp_{dyplhkev+vebnlpsy} \), where the subscripts are reckoned modulo \( fsmtiqro \).

The factorization
\[
vebnlpsy^{fsmtiqro}-1=(vebnlpsy-1)\left(vebnlpsy^{fsmtiqro-1}+vebnlpsy^{fsmtiqro-2}+\cdots+1\right)
\]
gives
\[
\prod_{uplnsciv=1}^{fsmtiqro-1}\left(vebnlpsy-qzxwvtnp_{uplnsciv}\right)=vebnlpsy^{fsmtiqro-1}+vebnlpsy^{fsmtiqro-2}+\cdots+1
\]
and in particular
\[
\prod_{uplnsciv=1}^{fsmtiqro-1}\left(1-qzxwvtnp_{uplnsciv}\right)=fsmtiqro .
\]

For a moment let \( gahovrte \) be a fixed index. Then \( \left|hjgrksla-pfqnbxci\right|=\left|1-qzxwvtnp_{wbzepkqd-gahovrte}\right| \), and therefore
\[
\prod_{wbzepkqd \neq gahovrte}\left|hjgrksla-pfqnbxci\right|=\prod_{uplnsciv=1}^{fsmtiqro-1}\left|1-qzxwvtnp_{uplnsciv}\right|=fsmtiqro .
\]

Multiplying these equations for \( gahovrte=1,2, \ldots, fsmtiqro \), we obtain
\[
\prod_{gahovrte=1}^{fsmtiqro} \prod_{wbzepkqd \neq gahovrte}\left|hjgrksla-pfqnbxci\right|=fsmtiqro^{fsmtiqro} .
\]

Since each linear factor appears twice in this double product,
\[
\prod_{gahovrte<wbzepkqd}\left|hjgrksla-pfqnbxci\right|^{2}=fsmtiqro^{fsmtiqro} .
\]

Therefore the required product is \( jczowgyp\, fsmtiqro^{fsmtiqro} \), where \( jczowgyp \) is a number of absolute value one. We must evaluate \( jczowgyp \).

If \( gahovrte<wbzepkqd \) and \( gahovrte+wbzepkqd \neq fsmtiqro \), then \( \left(hjgrksla-pfqnbxci\right)^{2} \) and \( \left(nefgclir-ycxmbord\right)^{2} \) are distinct factors in the product. Since they are conjugates of one another, their product is a positive real number. The factors of this type can thus be paired off so that each pair has a positive product. The remaining factors are \( \left(hjgrksla-ycxmbord\right)^{2} \) where \( 1<gahovrte<fsmtiqro-gahovrte \). Since \( hjgrksla \) and \( ycxmbord \) are conjugate, each of these factors is a negative real number.

Suppose that \( fsmtiqro \) is odd. Then there are negative factors for \( gahovrte=1,2, \ldots, (fsmtiqro-1) / 2 \), so \( jczowgyp=(-1)^{(fsmtiqro-1) / 2}=(-1)^{(fsmtiqro-1)(fsmtiqro-2) / 2} \).
Suppose \( fsmtiqro \) is even. Then there are negative factors for \( gahovrte=1,2, \ldots, (fsmtiqro-2) / 2 \), so \( jczowgyp=(-1)^{(fsmtiqro-2) / 2}=(-1)^{(fsmtiqro-1)(fsmtiqro-2) / 2} \).

Thus, in either case
\[
\prod_{gahovrte<wbzepkqd}\left(hjgrksla-pfqnbxci\right)^{2}=(-1)^{(fsmtiqro-1)(fsmtiqro-2) / 2}\, fsmtiqro^{fsmtiqro}
\]

Remark. The product of the squares of the differences of the zeros of a polynomial is known as the discriminant of the polynomial.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 be an integer and let k be a positive integer with 1 \leq  k \leq  n-1 and gcd(k,n)=1.  
Denote by \zeta _1, \ldots , \zeta _n the n complex roots of  

  t^n - 3 = 0.

Evaluate the closed-form value of  

  P_k := \prod _{1 \leq  j<i\leq n} (\zeta _i^k - \zeta _j^k)^2.
kernel_variant.solution:
Paragraph 1.  Write the roots of t^n-3 in a more convenient shape.  Let  
\omega _1, \ldots , \omega _n be the n th roots of unity (\omega _m := e^{2\pi im/n}).  Since 3 has one fixed real n th root 3^{1/n}, every root of t^n-3 equals  

  \zeta _i = 3^{1/n} \omega _i  (i = 1,\ldots ,n).

Paragraph 2.  Our target product contains the k th powers of the \zeta _i.  
Because gcd(k,n)=1, exponentiation by k permutes the \omega _i:

  {\omega _1^k, \ldots , \omega _n^k} = {\omega _1, \ldots , \omega _n}.                           (1)

Paragraph 3.  First recall the classical product for the plain roots of unity.  
For the polynomial t^n-1, factorisation gives  

  t^n-1 = (t-\omega _1)\ldots (t-\omega _n).

Hence \omega _1\omega _2\ldots \omega _n = (-1)^{n-1}.                                   (2)

Paragraph 4.  Differentiate t^n-1:

  n t^{n-1} = \sum _{i=1}^{n} \prod _{j\neq i}(t-\omega _j).

Evaluating at t = \omega _i we get

  n \omega _i^{\,n-1} = \prod _{j\neq i}(\omega _i-\omega _j)  (i=1,\ldots ,n).              (3)

Paragraph 5.  Multiply (3) over i=1,\ldots ,n.  
The left-hand side becomes n^n(\omega _1\ldots \omega _n)^{n-1}.  
Using (2) this equals n^n(-1)^{(n-1)^2}.  
The right-hand side is each linear difference twice, so

  n^n(-1)^{(n-1)^2} = (-1)^{n(n-1)/2} \prod _{j<i}(\omega _i-\omega _j)^2.

After a routine parity check one arrives at

  \prod _{j<i}(\omega _i-\omega _j)^2 = (-1)^{(n-1)(n-2)/2} n^n.                (4)

Paragraph 6.  Now attack the desired product.  
For each unordered pair (i,j) with j<i,

  \zeta _i^k-\zeta _j^k = (3^{1/n}\omega _i)^k - (3^{1/n}\omega _j)^k  
       = 3^{k/n}(\omega _i^k-\omega _j^k).                               (5)

Paragraph 7.  There are n(n-1)/2 such pairs.  
Pulling out the common factor 3^{k/n} from (5) for every pair contributes the global constant

  3^{(k/n)\cdot (n(n-1)/2)} = 3^{k(n-1)/2}.                      (6)

Paragraph 8.  Square the differences as required in P_k; the constant from (6) therefore squares to

  3^{k(n-1)}.                                                (7)

Paragraph 9.  Thanks to permutation property (1), replacing each \omega _i by \omega _i^k merely re-orders the factors in (4).  Consequently

  \prod _{j<i}(\omega _i^k-\omega _j^k)^2 = \prod _{j<i}(\omega _i-\omega _j)^2.                    (8)

Paragraph 10.  Combining (4), (7) and (8) we finally get

  P_k = 3^{k(n-1)} \cdot  (-1)^{(n-1)(n-2)/2} n^n.                (9)

Paragraph 11.  Because the exponent (n-1)(n-2)/2 is always integral, expression (9) is the promised closed form.  Observe that the answer reduces to the ordinary discriminant of t^n-3 when k=1.

Answer.  
  P_k = (-1)^{(n-1)(n-2)/2} n^n 3^{k(n-1)}.

_replacement_note.reason: Original kernel variant was too easy compared to the original problem
